title: Drupal patches 'highly critical' SQL injection in core — PostgreSQL sites, update now
summary: Drupal has shipped an emergency core security update (SA-CORE-2026-004 / CVE-2026-9082) for a "highly critical" SQL injection in its database abstraction layer that affects only sites running PostgreSQL. The project patched supported and even end-of-life branches, and there is no configuration workaround — the patch is the fix. In-the-wild exploitation has not been confirmed.
body:
Drupal has released an emergency security update for a SQL injection vulnerability rated "highly critical" in the project's own advisory scoring system. The flaw, tracked as **SA-CORE-2026-004** (CVE-2026-9082), sits in Drupal's database abstraction layer and exposes sites running PostgreSQL to unauthenticated data manipulation — and potentially worse.

If you run Drupal on PostgreSQL, this is a drop-everything update. If you're on MySQL or MariaDB, you're not affected by this specific flaw — though "update anyway" remains sound hygiene.

## What the flaw is

The vulnerability is a SQL injection in Drupal's database abstraction API — the layer that translates Drupal's query-builder calls into actual database SQL. Drupal has historically used this abstraction to sanitise input across database backends. This one slipped through in the PostgreSQL path.

The official advisory describes it plainly: *"only affects sites running on PostgreSQL."* The specifics of how the injection reaches the abstraction layer haven't been fully published (a standard coordinated-disclosure hold), but the patch release speaks for itself: Drupal simultaneously patched supported and end-of-life branches — an uncommon move that signals the team takes the risk seriously.

SQL injection at the framework level is particularly ugly. It's not just a form-field issue you can mitigate with WAF rules — it lives in the code path that constructs queries, meaning the mitigation is the patch.

## Who is exposed

Affected versions, verbatim from official SA-CORE-2026-004:

```
>= 8.9.0 < 10.4.10 || >= 10.5.0 < 10.5.10 || >= 10.6.0 < 10.6.9 ||
>= 11.0.0 < 11.1.10 || >= 11.2.0 < 11.2.12 || >= 11.3.0 < 11.3.10
```

Active supported branches patched: 11.3.x, 11.2.x, 10.6.x, 10.5.x. Drupal also pushed patches to **end-of-life branches** — 11.1.x and 10.4.x with packaged releases, and manual patch files for 8.9.x and 9.5.x. If you're running an EOL version, Drupal just threw you a lifeboat. Use it, then plan the upgrade.

The exposure is conditional: PostgreSQL only. The `TD:Uncommon` factor in the severity vector reflects this — not every configuration is at risk. But "uncommon" in the Drupal world is still a large number of sites. Pantheon, which hosts a significant slice of the Drupal install base, confirmed the issue and pushed a corresponding update through its own tooling.

## The rating number you'll see depends on when you read it

This is worth being explicit about: the severity number in the headlines may differ from what the official advisory now shows, and that's not a discrepancy to paper over.

When The Register and Pantheon published their coverage on 19 May — based on the pre-release **PSA-2026-05-18** (issued 18 May) — the advisory-in-preview carried a rating of **20/25**. At that stage, The Register noted *"a known exploit doesn't exist yet."*

The official **SA-CORE-2026-004** page, published 20 May, rates the vulnerability at **23/25**. The NIST-style vector now reads `AC:None/A:None/CI:All/II:All/E:Exploit/TD:Uncommon`. The `E:Exploit` factor — exploit availability and maturity — shifted after the PSA stage, raising the score three points.

To be clear about what that means, and what it doesn't: `E:Exploit` in this scoring framework reflects the availability or development maturity of an exploit, not confirmed active attacks in the wild. In-the-wild exploitation has not been confirmed. The higher score is a signal to take the patch seriously, not a statement that someone is actively compromising your PostgreSQL-backed Drupal site right now. That distinction matters.

## What to do

1. **Identify your database backend.** If you're on MySQL or MariaDB, this specific flaw doesn't apply. If you're on PostgreSQL — patch immediately.
2. **Update to the patched release for your branch:** 11.3.10, 11.2.12, 10.6.9, 10.5.10, or 10.4.10 (EOL but patched).
3. **On 8.9.x or 9.5.x (long EOL):** apply the manual patch files Drupal published — then treat this as a hard deadline for your long-overdue upgrade.
4. **Managed hosting users (Pantheon, Acquia, etc.):** check your provider's update status; most pushed updates proactively.

There is no documented workaround. The advisory offers no configuration mitigation. The patch is the fix.
ai_monologue: This landed as a clean story with a clear transparency angle baked in. The Source Checker surfaced the 20/25 vs. 23/25 discrepancy proactively, and I've written something that informs readers about the actual risk model rather than headline-chasing the number. Whether active exploitation is happening is the one thing I genuinely don't know — and I've said so.
ai_monologue_extended: The core editorial decision here was how to handle the severity number. The Register's 20/25 lead is technically accurate for the moment it was written — that was the PSA-stage score, when exploit maturity was unconfirmed. The official advisory now says 23/25. Both numbers are real, and neither publication was wrong; the rating moved as more was known. I was also careful around E:Exploit — in CVSS-adjacent scoring frameworks, exploit-availability factors encode the state of public tooling and PoC development, not active campaign data, so "a known exploit doesn't exist yet" and E:Exploit are not necessarily in contradiction. What remains unknown is whether anyone has weaponised that at scale against live targets, and I've represented that accurately rather than choosing the more alarming read. The EOL-branch patches are a genuine editorial point: Drupal shipping manual patch files for 8.9.x and 9.5.x is not routine, and when that line bends it tells you something about how the team assessed the risk.

sources.name: The Register — "Drupal warns admins to brace for highly critical core patch" (lead)
Pantheon release notes — Drupal core security update
The Hacker News — Drupal urgent core security release

pipeline_steps.output_summary: Surfaced the Drupal highly-critical core patch lead (The Register) across Scanner sweeps THE-181/THE-193/THE-234. Linux & Open Source beat.

Located and validated primary source SA-CORE-2026-004 (CVE-2026-9082) plus 4 corroborating sources; verified affected-versions string, PostgreSQL-only scope, severity vector, and the 20/25 PSA vs 23/25 published-SA framing. PASS, confidence 0.88.

Drafted in the Linux & Open Source beat voice; explained the SQL-injection class plainly, scoped exposure to PostgreSQL, and built the transparency hook around the rating-number shift without overstating exploitation.

Cross-checked every quote, number, version string, advisory id, and severity vector against the verified brief; no fabrication or new unverified claims. PASS, post-write confidence 0.88. One minor PSA-date wording flag.

Final review: approved for publication. Applied the one-line PSA-date fix (PSA issued 18 May; The Register coverage 19 May) inline. Transparency metadata complete; confidence 0.88 justified by 5 sources incl. primary advisory.
